1: kephalaion de epi tois legomenois, toiouton echomen archierea, hos ekathisen en dexia tou thronou tēs megalōsynēs en tois ouranois — "now the point in what we are saying is this: we have such a high priest, one who is seated at the right hand of the throne of the Majesty in heaven" (kephalaion: the main point, summary, head of the argument — not merely a summary but the key point that the whole argument has been building toward): tēs megalōsynēs (of the Majesty): a circumlocution for God, common in Jewish reverence-language. Ekathisen (sat down — aorist: the completed seating): the seated high priest, the completed work.
5: hoitines hypodeigmati kai skia latreuousin tōn epouraniōn, kathōs kechrēmatistai Mōysēs mellōn epitelein tēn skēnēn — "who serve a copy and shadow of the heavenly things. For when Moses was about to erect the tent, he was instructed by God, saying: See that you make everything according to the pattern that was shown you on the mountain" (hypodeigmati kai skia: copy/example and shadow — both terms indicate derivativeness: the earthly sanctuary is not the original but its earthly reproduction; kechrēmatistai: has been warned/instructed by divine oracle — the same verb used for Joseph being warned in a dream, Matthew 2:12): the divine instruction proves the earthly sanctuary is intentionally modeled on a heavenly reality.
6: nyn de diaphorōteras tetychēken leitourgias, hosō kai kreittōnos estin diathēkēs mesitēs, hētis epi kreittōsi epangeliais nenomothetētai — "but as it is, Christ has obtained a ministry that is as much more excellent than the old as the covenant he mediates is better, since it is enacted on better promises" (diaphorōteras leitourgias tetychēken: has obtained a more excellent ministry — diaphoros: different, superior; mesitēs: mediator, middle-person between two parties; nenomothetētai: has been legislated/established — the perfect passive indicates the settled legal establishment of the new covenant): the comparative structure: as much better as the covenant is better, as much as the promises are better — a chain of comparatives.
8: mempthomenos gar autous legei, Idou, hēmerai erchontai, legei Kyrios — "for he finds fault with them when he says: Behold, the days are coming, declares the Lord. (mempthomenos autous: finding fault with them — the "them" are the people who made the first covenant inadequate by their failure): the author's reading of Jer 31 is that the new covenant promise itself implies a critique of the old: God would not promise a new covenant if the old had been without fault. The Jeremiah citation frames the promise as a divine critique-and-solution to the first covenant's demonstrated inadequacy.
9: hoti ouk estai kata tēn diathēkēn hēn epoiēsa tois patrasin autōn en hēmera epilabomenou mou tēs cheiros autōn exagagein autous ek gēs Aigyptou, hoti autoi ouk enemeinan en tē diathēkē mou, kagō ēmelēsa autōn, legei Kyrios — "not like the covenant that I made with their fathers on the day when I took them by the hand to bring them out of the land of Egypt. For they did not continue in my covenant, and so I showed no concern for them" (ouk enemeinan en tē diathēkē: they did not remain in the covenant — the first covenant's failure is not God's failure of design but Israel's failure of faithfulness; kagō ēmelēsa autōn: and I showed no concern for them — the divine response is corresponding covenant-withdrawal).
13: en tō legein Kainēn pepalaiōken tēn prōtēn; to de palaioumenon kai gēraskon engys aphanismou — "in speaking of a new covenant, he makes the first one obsolete. And what is becoming obsolete and growing old is ready to vanish away" (pepalaiōken: has made old — perfect active: the declaration of newness accomplished the obsolescence of the old): the kainēn (new — qualitatively new, not just recent) is the key: when Scripture uses kainēn, it implies the oldness of what it replaces. Engys aphanismou (near disappearance) — the present tense palaioumenon and gēraskon (becoming old, aging) suggest the process was underway at the time of writing; the Jerusalem temple's destruction (70 CE) would be the visible completion.
2: tōn hagiōn leitourgos kai tēs skēnēs tēs alēthinēs, hēn epēxen ho kyrios, ouk anthrōpos — "who serves in the sanctuary, the true tabernacle set up by the Lord, not by a mere human being" (leitourgos: liturgist, one who performs public religious service — a priestly/cultic technical term; tōn hagiōn leitourgos: liturgist of the holy things/sanctuary): tēs skēnēs tēs alēthinēs (the true tabernacle — alēthinēs: true, genuine, the original of which others are copies): the alēthinos/alēthēs distinction: alēthinos means "genuine, real, as opposed to copies and shadows" rather than merely "truthful." The earthly tabernacle is a copy; this one is the original. Hēn epēxen ho kyrios, ouk anthrōpos (which the Lord pitched, not a human being): the divine construction establishes its nature — not constructed within the created order.
3: pas gar archiereus eis to prospherein dōra te kai thysias kathistatai; hothen anagkaion echein ti kai touton ho prospherē — "every high priest is appointed to offer both gifts and sacrifices, and so it was necessary for this one also to have something to offer" (eis to prospherein: for the purpose of offering — the infinitive of purpose defines the essential function of the high priest): anagkaion (necessary): the logical necessity follows from the universal: since every high priest must offer, and since Christ is a high priest, Christ must also have an offering. The offering cannot be the repeated animal sacrifices of the Levitical system — his offering is identified as his own self (Hebrews 9:14, 26).
4: ei men oun ēn epi gēs, oud' an ēn hiereus, ontōn tōn kata nomon proscheron ōra ta dōra — "if he were on earth, he would not be a priest at all, since there are priests who offer the gifts prescribed by the law" (ei men oun ēn epi gēs: if indeed he were on earth — second-class conditional, contrary to fact: the present condition is that Christ is NOT on earth): the argument is about jurisdictional categories: the earthly sphere already has its established priestly order. Christ's priesthood operates in the heavenly sphere — not competing with the Levitical system on its own terrain but fulfilling the reality of which the earthly is only the copy.
7: ei gar hē prōtē ekeinē ēn amemptos, ouk an deuteras ezēteito topos — "for if there had been nothing wrong with that first covenant, no place would have been sought for another" (ei... ēn amemptos: if it had been blameless — second-class conditional, contrary to fact; amemptos: blameless, without fault): the argument from the existence of Jer 31's new covenant promise: God would not promise what was already provided. The existence of the promise is itself evidence of the first covenant's inadequacy. Amemptos does not imply the first covenant was wrong or evil — but that it was incomplete and incapable of achieving the final goal.
10: hoti autē hē diathēkē hēn diathēsomai tō oikō Israēl meta tas hēmeras ekeinasmeta tas hēmeras ekeinasdidonai nomous mou eis tēn dianoian autōn, kai epi kardias autōn epigrapsō autous, kai esomai autois eis theon kai autoi esontai moi eis laon — "I will put my laws in their minds and write them on their hearts. I will be their God, and they will be my people" (Jeremiah 31:33 LXX): didonai nomous eis tēn dianoian (giving laws into the mind — dianoia: the faculty of thinking, the mind as the organ of understanding): and epi kardias epigrapsō (I will write upon their hearts — epigrapsō: to write upon, to engrave on): the new covenant's law is internalized — not external tablets but internal inscription. Dianoia (mind/understanding) and kardia (heart) together cover the whole inner person. The covenant formula esomai autois eis theon... autoi... eis laon (I will be to them as God, they will be to me as a people) is the bedrock covenantal language from the Sinai tradition — the new covenant fulfills the same relational goal.
11: kai ou mē didaxōsin hekastos ton politen autou kai hekastos ton adelphon autou legōn, Gnōthi ton Kyrion, hoti pantes eidēsousin me apo mikrou heōs megalou autōn — "no longer will they teach their neighbor, or say to one another, Know the Lord, because they will all know me, from the least of them to the greatest" (ou mē... didaxōsin: they shall by no means teach — the double negative ou mē + aorist subjunctive is the strongest Greek negation): Gnōthi ton Kyrion (Know the Lord): in the first covenant, external instruction mediated the knowledge of God through priests and prophets; in the new covenant, the knowledge is direct and universal. Pantes eidēsousin me (all shall know me — oida: the perfect-present knowledge that is settled and complete): from the smallest to the greatest — the knowledge transcends social and educational stratification.
12: hoti hileōs esomai tais adikiais autōn kai tōn hamartiōn autōn ou mē mnēsthō eti — "for I will forgive their wickedness and will remember their sins no more" (Jeremiah 31:34 LXX): hileōs esomai (I will be merciful/gracious — hileōs: propitious, gracious, disposed to forgive; the word used for the Day of Atonement mercy): ou mē mnēsthō eti (I will by no means remember any longer — double negative ou mē + aorist subjunctive + eti: the most emphatic possible negation): the divine "forgetting" is not cognitive failure but an act of deliberate covenant release — God will not bring up the forgiven sins as legal evidence. This is the culminating promise of the new covenant: the basis of everything that precedes it in the Jeremiah citation is this final act of complete forgiveness.
